description.en: A generalised ontology for manufacturing quality troubleshooting, abstracting from the die-casting domain. Covers both the specified (schema-level) and actual (instance-level) layers of a manufacturing process. This ontology generalizes the patterns established in the die-casting ontologies (dg.ttl and dga.ttl) to be applicable across different manufacturing domains, with a focus on root cause analysis, failure diagnosis, and countermeasure recommendation.

comments.en: Relationship to Procedural Knowledge Ontology (PKO): mqto countermeasure classes align with PKO procedure modeling patterns. mqto:Countermeasure is a subclass of pko:Procedure, representing remediation procedures in the specified layer. mqto:CountermeasureStep aligns with p-plan:Step for individual actions. In the actual layer, mqto:CountermeasureExecution extends pko:ProcedureExecution and mqto:CountermeasureStepExecution extends pko:StepExecution. Both ontologies follow a specification/execution (plan/actual) pattern, though mqto uses LIS (lis:Specified/lis:Actual with lis:implements) while PKO uses P-PLAN/PROV (with pko:executesProcedure/pko:executesStep). Key architectural difference: mqto externalizes step ordering via mqto:List containers for step reusability across procedures, while PKO embeds ordering directly in steps via pko:nextStep properties.

abstract.value: The Manufacturing Quality Troubleshooting Ontology (MQTO) provides a domain-independent semantic framework for modeling manufacturing quality inspection, failure diagnosis, and countermeasure recommendation. Building on the LIS (Lifecycle Information System) specified/actual pattern and integrating SOSA/SSN observation semantics, MQTO enables structured representation of Ishikawa (fishbone) root cause analysis across Machine, Material, and Method categories. The ontology supports both schema-level knowledge (failure types, metrics, countermeasure procedures) and instance-level data (actual observations, failure occurrences, executed remediation steps), making it applicable across diverse manufacturing domains including die-casting, injection molding, machining, and assembly processes.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A spatial location that is a three-dimensional volume.

primitiveRationale.value: A comprehensive account of geometries is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: A volume designated for 3D engineering at a point in time, a box-shaped volume determined by coordinates in a 3D frame of reference; the extent of a gas leak at 12:00

definition.value: Any information that is reported from a Procedure.

comment.en: Any information that is reported from a Procedure.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A continuous period of time.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of temporal intervals is beyond the scope of IDO (temporal logic offers no obvious consensus).

example.value: The time an instrument has been in operation, the time during which a measurement was made.

comment.undefined: 'interval' is defined in OWL Time as "A temporal entity with an extent or duration".

usageNote.value: This class is not intended to be used directly, but only for serving as the domain of 'implements' and similar relations. The disjointness axiom serves to block iterated chains of 'implements', etc., relationships.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An entity that is part of an engineering specification.

primitiveRationale.value: The notion of specification is not defined. An explication should be given in terms of systems engineering practices.

example.value: A "tag" individual, a function of a tag, an activity as characterised in process design.

comment.undefined: A 'specified' entity is typically an individual that represents the asset as designed, with restrictions that capture requirements.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A 'physical object' that is not living.

primitiveRationale.value: An account of what "living" means is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: A tool, a building, a vehicle, an instrument, a parking lot.

comment.undefined: ISO 15926-2:2003, p. 122: "A class_of_inanimate_physical_object is a class_of_arranged\-_individual whose members are not living."

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A quality that can be characterised in terms of geometry.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of geometries is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The flat shape of a surface feature, the cylindrical shape of a piece of pipe.

usageNote.value: The class 'stream' is intended to be used for flows of material or energy, in accordance with conventional use. There is an abundance of cases where use of the term "stream" might be called into question, as in, is a stream of electricity really moving; how many cars are needed to participate before it is properly called a "flow" of cars; while a flow of sand is a stream, maybe a succession of rolling rocks would not qualify.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An inanimate physical object consisting of energy or homogeneous parts that flow through a system.

primitiveRationale.value: A full account of the criteria for what counts as a stream is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: A stream of fluid in a pipeline, electricity in an electrical wire, ore moving on a conveyor belt.

comment.undefined: ISO 15926-2:2003, p. 112, states, "A stream is a physical_object that is material or energy moving along a path, where the path is the basis of identity and may be constrained. The stream consists of the temporal parts of those things that are in the stream whilst they are in it."

definition.value: An actuatable quality (property, characteristic) of a FeatureOfInterest.

example.value: A window actuator acts by changing the state between a frame and a window. The ability of the window to be opened and closed is its ActuatableProperty.

comment.en: An actuatable quality (property, characteristic) of a FeatureOfInterest.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An entity that is not dependent on other entities for existence, and which persists through time.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of what counts as an 'object' is beyond the scope of IDO.

scopeNote.value: In IDO, ’information object’ is represented as a subclass of ’object’. According to the BFO literature, and applied in the Information Artifact Ontology and other BFO compliant ontologies, information artifacts are (multiply) dependent objects. a /sibling/ class to ’independent continuant’ rather than a subclass, as used in IDO.

example.value: A process plant, a compressor, a wire, a person, an operator, a database record, an industry standard, a manual document, a site.

comment.undefined: From the DOLCE description of 'endurant': "The main characteristic of endurants is that all of them are independent essential wholes. ..."

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A spatial location consisting of one or more lines.

primitiveRationale.value: A comprehensive account of geometries is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: A line connecting two points with coordinates relative to the frame of reference, the path of a flexible pipe at 12:00 AM.

usageNote.value: The utility of a 'site' may be represented by assigning one or more 'role's, such as 'front desk role', 'storage space role', or 'entryway role'.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A location determined in relation to one or more physical objects.

primitiveRationale.value: A comprehensive definition of the notion of demarcation is beyond the scope of IDO.

scopeNote.value: The position of a 'site' follows the position of the 'physical object' by which it is demarcated.
Locations such as countries and cities are also considered sites.

example.value: A flight deck, the inside of a manifold, the hold of a ship, a centre of gravity, an outside area

comment.undefined: This class is inspired by the BFO class 'site'.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A physical object that is living.

primitiveRationale.value: Providing precise semantics of what "living thing" amounts to is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: Animal, plant, fungus, bacterium.

usageNote.value: IDO is an anthropocentric ontology in the sense that it is disregards complexities of the deep theories of the physical sciences that have only negligible impact at the standard industrial scale. For example, this means ignoring cosmological effects on the average industry and adopting a geocentric model of the universe where there exists a commonly agreed upon stationary earthly “spatial reference system”.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An object that is a region in space.

primitiveRationale.value: A comprehensive definition of the notion of location is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: An outdoor plant area, an opening in a wall, the space inside an enclosure

definition.value: An Actuation carries out an (Actuation) Procedure to change the state of the world using an Actuator.

example.value: The activity of automatically closing a window if the temperature in a room drops below 20 degree Celsius. The activity is the Actuation and the device that closes the window is the Actuator. The Procedure is the rule, plan, or specification that defines the conditions that triggers the Actuation, here a drop in temperature.

comment.en: An Actuation carries out an (Actuation) Procedure to change the state of the world using an Actuator.

usageNote.value: Qualities are in general subject to change over the lifetime of the bearer. The main IDO device for representing quality changes is through 'datum' individuals: see lis:qualityQuantifiedAs.

Qualities should only be used for root properties such as voltage, pressure, etc. Where there are variants such as nominal, rated, actual voltage, modellers should use lis:Quality for voltage and use a subclass of lis:QualityDatum for the moderator ("nominal", etc.)

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A dependent entity that is present any time its bearer exists.

explanatoryNote.value: In ISO 15926-2:2003, the term "quality or characteristic" is used to explain what the entity type 'property' represents (clause 5.2.26.5, notes 1 and 2). Note 2 reads, "The types of characteristic or quality, such as temperature or density, are instances of class_of_property."

primitiveRationale.value: Qualities cover a broad range of what is commonly referred to as "properties". It is beyond the scope of IDO to precisely determine what should be included.

example.value: The temperature of a fluid stream, the tensile strength of a bolt, the length of a pipeline, the colour of a surface coating, the familiarity of a specialist with a modification procedure, the quality of a window of being open or closed.

comment.undefined: SSN offers the following as an example for ActuatableProperty, a subclass of Property: "A window actuator acts by changing the state between a frame and a window. The ability of the window to be opened and closed is its ActuatableProperty."

usageNote.value: 'unit of measure' may be used when there is ambiguity about the scale of the measure.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An information object that defines the magnitude of a 'quality', adopted by convention or by law.

primitiveRationale.value: Precisely determining what qualifies as a unit of measure is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: carton, cup, temperature degree, instances of scales

comment.undefined: 'unit' is a class in the Ontology of units of Measure (OM 2).

comment.en: Actual-layer counterpart of Signal. Represents a property (observable or actuable) of an ActualIshikawaItem that implements a Signal specification from the specified layer. While Signal defines the data channel metadata (identifier, unit, sampling rate, direction), Property represents the actual measured or actuated quality of a specific feature of interest. This class serves as the abstract superclass for both ObservableProperty (for sensing/measurement) and ActuableProperty (for actuation/control).

A reusable specification of a single step within a Countermeasure procedure. Because the same step (e.g. 'clean the mould') may occur in multiple Countermeasures, ordering is not encoded in the step itself but in the mqto:List that references it. As a subclass of p-plan:Step, countermeasure steps align with the P-PLAN ontology for plan-based activity modeling.

Subclass of sosa:Observation, representing a single sensor reading or measurement event. Links to an ActualIshikawaItem (feature of interest), an ObservableProperty (what is being measured), and a Result (the measured value). In die-casting (dga ontology), observations capture time-series data from PLCs, sensors, and SCADA systems - e.g., furnace temperature readings, die pressure measurements, cycle times.

definition.value: An act of Sampling carries out a sampling Procedure to create or transform one or more samples.

example.value: Registering an image of the landscape.
Drawing blood from a patient.
Selecting a subset of a population.
Crushing a rock sample in a ball mill.
Establishing a station for environmental monitoring.
Dividing a field site into quadrants.
Drilling an observation well.
Splitting a piece of drill-core to create two new samples.
Taking a diamond-drill core from a rock outcrop.
Digging a pit through a soil sequence.
Sieving a powder to separate the subset finer than 100-mesh.

comment.en: An act of Sampling carries out a sampling Procedure to create or transform one or more samples.

usageNote.value: Quantification may be on nominal, ordinal, and other scales.

This class may be used for data that don't fit the restriction to "single unit, single number" of 'scalar quantity datum', including data employing vectors instead of numbers, and conventional categories like "high" and "medium".

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An information object that quantifies a quality.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of the  representation of magnitudes is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: 10 volts, high (of pressure), low (of temperature), category 3 (as used to rate the strength 'quality' of a storm), 3 MB (a file size), qualifications, grades.

scopeNote.value: Introduce subclasses of 'quality datum' in RDL to distinguish categories of data, such as nominal versus measured values.

comment.undefined: IAO_0000109 is 'measurement datum', a class in the Information Artifact Ontology.

definition.value: Describes the Deployment of one or more Systems for a particular purpose. Deployment may be done on a Platform.

example.value: For example, a temperature Sensor deployed on a wall, or a whole network of Sensors deployed for an Observation campaign.

comment.en: Describes the Deployment of one or more Systems for a particular purpose. Deployment may be done on a Platform.

definition.value: The Result of an Observation, Actuation, or act of Sampling. To store an observation's simple result value one can use the hasSimpleResult property.

example.value: The value 20 as the height of a certain tree together with the unit, e.g., Meter.

comment.en: The Result of an Observation, Actuation, or act of Sampling. To store an observation's simple result value one can use the hasSimpleResult property.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An activity  that occurs at one or more specific points in time.

example.value: The beginning of a lifting operation, the end of a measurement activity, the midpoint between beginning and end of an activity.

comment.undefined: An 'event' doesn't need to be temporally connected. For example, several instances of switching on a heater over a week may be represented by a single 'event' individual; it will occupy a non-singular 'instant region'. The details of a singular event part may be modelled by relating the event to 'instant' individuals.

usageNote.value: Installed software is considered a physical object.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An object that occupies space and possesses mass.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of what it means to be a physical object is beyond the scope of IDO. There is no obvious consensus on the notion of "material".

example.value: A wall, a fluid stream, a welder, a printed manual, a seafloor.

comment.undefined: IDO asserts that 'physical object' and 'activity' are disjunct classes. In contrast to this, and according to a 4D approach, ISO 15926-2:2003, p. 26, states that "physical_object and activity are not mutually exclusive. A possible_individual can be both".

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An instant region with no proper parts

primitiveRationale.value: (Could be considered non-primitive, defined in terms of 'instant region' and 'no proper part'.)

example.value: The moment a valve is closed, the time of a measurement as use for time-stamping, the moment an activity starts.

comment.undefined: 'instant' is defined in OWL Time as "A temporal entity with zero extent or duration".

usageNote.value: Renamed class. Checked materially in Oxford English Dictionary.
A typical RDL includes classes that categorise individuals according to material composition. This class is suitable as the top node of such a taxonomy.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A physical object with a defined material composition.

primitiveRationale.value: The semantics of materials, of which compounds would be a specialisation, is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: Types of material composition, such as 316 stainless steel, reinforced concrete, and carbon monoxide.

scopeNote.value: Natural kinds are expressly not to be classified as 'compound'. The class 'compound' is only to be used to classify amounts of matter, with non-semantic auxiliaries used to refer to chemical composition. The semantics of natural kinds such as "helium", "steel", or "hydrocarbon" is out of scope for IDO.

comment.en: An observable property of an ActualIshikawaItem that can be measured via Observations. As a subclass of mqto:Property, it implements a Signal specification from the specified layer, establishing traceability from actual observations back to the specified data channel definition. Note: subclassing both sosa:ObservableProperty (an abstract quality type) and lis:Actual (via mqto:Property, a concrete instance) reflects the modelling choice that each mqto:ObservableProperty individual represents a specific, concrete observable quality tied to a particular actual item — not a reusable abstract type. This parallels how sosa:ObservableProperty instances are used in practice (e.g. 'the temperature of this specific machine part') rather than as universal property types.

usageNote.value: Most artefacts are both physical and functional objects as a functional object is any object that has a function.

A password must be concretized, this is done when it is entered, the password performs the function of unlocking the computer. The password participates in the unlocking process through its concretization.
Note that it is possible to model without explicitly using this class, as any object that has a function can be inferred to be a functional object.
This class can be used as a root class for functional classification of objects, including physical objects.
A "tag", when included in an IDO asset model, should in general have a type that is subclass of both 'functional object' and 'physical artefact'.

Artefact types tend to be described primarily in terms of 'function'; for example, the "minimal" definition of a pump would be, "a physical artefact, the function of which is to pump (to be the active participant in a pumping activity)". This kind of definition places no restriction on physical make-up and is suitable for a subclass of 'functional object'. Note that the activity type of 'pumping' is here a precursor to the definition of the physical object type 'pump'. A taxonomy of pump classes should extend from a class 'pump' defined solely in terms of function, as above.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An 'Object' that provides a required 'Function'.

semiFormalNaturalLanguageAxiom.value: If x is a 'functional object', then x is an 'object' that 'has function' some 'Function'.

example.value: A pump, a hammer, a password.

comment.undefined: ISO 15926-2:2003, p. 110, states, "A functional_physical_object is a physical_object that has functional, rather than material, continuity as its basis for identity. Adjacent temporal parts of a functional_physical_object need not have common matter or energy, provided the matter or energy of each temporal part fulfils the same function."

usageNote.value: An RDL can include classes that categorise individuals according to material phase. This class is suitable as the top node of such a taxonomy.
Definition from Oxford English Dictionary.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A physical object with a distinct and homogeneous form.

primitiveRationale.value: The semantics of phases is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: Solid object, liquid object, gaseous object.

usageNote.value: A scalar quantity datum has a unique unit of measure and a unique numeric value. This class is inspired by the class "scalar measurement datum" of the Information Artifact Ontology.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A quality datum that quantifies a physical quantity with a numeric value and a scale.

semiFormalNaturalLanguageAxiom.value: If x is a 'scalar quantity datum', then x is a 'quality datum' which 'quantifies quality' some 'physical quantity', and has a 'datum uom' relationship to a 'unit of measure' and a 'datum value' relationship to a literal value.

example.value: 10 kilograms, 220 volt.

comment.undefined: IAO_0000032 is 'scalar measurement datum', a class in the Information Artifact Ontology.

usageNote.value: OWL data ranges can capture the acceptable numerical range for a scale. For instance, the 'datum value' of any 'quality datum' that has Celsius as 'datum uom' can be limited to the range from -273.15 upwards to infinity.
'scale' corresponds to the common sense notion of unit of measure which unambiguously defines the magnitude of the measure.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A unit of measure that, when paired with a number, unambiguously describes the magnitude of a physical quantity.

primitiveRationale.value: A comprehensive definition of the notion of scale is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: centimetre, kilogram per minute, degree Celsius, lunar distance, kilo-lines of code

scopeNote.value: Arguably, IDO conflates the notions of "unit of measure" and "scale" in an unhealthy way. While a unit of measure is a definite magnitude, a scale arises from fixing a number range to express multiples of the unit of measure. One may argue that a unit of measure has an implicit scale, obtained by multiplying the unit by any number; this will then allow for undefined or meaningless expressions like "minus 10 Kelvin".

comment.undefined: QUDT provides the following subclasses of 'scale': 'Enumeration scale', 'Interval scale', 'Nominal scale', 'Ordinal scale', 'Ratio scale'. Note that the notion of nominal scales diverges in being a coding scheme, with numbers not implying relative magnitude.

definition.value: A device that is used by, or implements, a Sampling Procedure to create or transform one or more samples.

example.value: A ball mill, diamond drill, hammer, hypodermic syringe and needle, image Sensor or a soil auger can all act as sampling devices (i.e., be Samplers). However, sometimes the distinction between the Sampler and the Sensor is not evident, as they are packaged as a unit. A Sampler need not be a physical device.

comment.en: A device that is used by, or implements, a Sampling Procedure to create or transform one or more samples.

An actuable property of an ActualIshikawaItem that can be controlled or modified via Actuations. As a subclass of mqto:Property, it implements a Signal specification from the specified layer. Represents properties that can be set or modified, such as setpoints, valve positions, motor speeds, or control commands. The Signal in the specified layer defines the control channel metadata, while ActuableProperty represents the actual controllable quality of a specific feature of interest.

The actual, single execution of one CountermeasureStep. Each execution implements a specific CountermeasureStep from the specified layer via lis:implements, performed in a concrete, non-reused linear order encoded via mqto:hasFirstStep / mqto:hasNextStep, contrasting with the specified layer where mqto:List enables step reuse across procedures. As a subclass of pko:StepExecution, countermeasure step executions align with PKO's step execution model. Note: Does not directly subclass lis:Activity (which is lis:Specified), but implements CountermeasureStep (which is lis:Activity), maintaining the LIS distinction between specified and actual layers.

Abstract superclass for the three categories of root causes in an Ishikawa (fishbone) diagram: Machine, Material, and Method. In die-casting ontologies (dg.ttl), this pattern is instantiated with dg:Aggregate (equivalent to Machine), dg:Material, and dg:Process (equivalent to Method), plus dg:Product (output of manufacturing, no direct mqto equivalent).

usageNote.value: The typical 'function' of a hammer is realized when the hammer participates as the tool in a successful nail-driving activity. If the hammer has multiple functions, such as extracting nails using a claw or shaping objects with a ball, each function should be represented with a distinct individual.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A capability that meets a design requirement.

primitiveRationale.value: Making the notion of meeting a requirement fully precise is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The function of a motor to apply a specified torque to a shaft.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000034 is 'function'.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A quality that can be characterised in terms of chemical or material properties.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of chemical properties is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The steel grade of a cast-iron component, the plastic type of a wire sheathing.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A disposition which when realized serves a positive or desired outcome.

primitiveRationale.value: Making the notion of "positive interest" fully precise is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The capability of a motor to generate power, of a pump to generate head and flow. The capability to use a hammer as a paperweight, which is not it's primary capability (function).

comment.undefined: NCIT_C74979 is 'capability'.

usageNote.value: A typical RDL includes classes that represent industrial item types, such as equipment, devices, assemblies, buildings, and products, among other elements. This class is suitable as the top node in a taxonomy of such classes.
The superclass of 'physical artefact' is not 'inanimate physical object', in order to allow for artificially made organisms.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A 'physical object' that has been created as the result of deliberate activity.

primitiveRationale.value: To give a precise account of "making" goes beyond the scope of IDO. An account would engage notions on which there is no clear consensus, including intentional notions of intended purpose, and of goal-directed action; of permanence and integrity; etc. There is an abundance of edge cases for "physical artefact".

example.value: a pressure transmitter, a process plant, a wrench, a computer.

definition.value: Act of carrying out an (Observation) Procedure to estimate or calculate a value of a property of a FeatureOfInterest. Links to a Sensor to describe what made the Observation and how; links to an ObservableProperty to describe what the result is an estimate of, and to a FeatureOfInterest to detail what that property was associated with.

example.value: The activity of estimating the intensity of an Earthquake using the Mercalli intensity scale is an Observation as is measuring the moment magnitude, i.e., the energy released by said earthquake.

comment.en: Act of carrying out an (Observation) Procedure to estimate or calculate a value of a property of a FeatureOfInterest. Links to a Sensor to describe what made the Observation and how; links to an ObservableProperty to describe what the result is an estimate of, and to a FeatureOfInterest to detail what that property was associated with.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An organism that is a human.

primitiveRationale.value: Providing precise semantics of what "human" amounts to is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: A human being.

scopeNote.value: In an RDL, subclasses of 'person' will typically require 'role' restrictions to be considered fully explicit. For example, 'maintenance engineer' will be a subclass of 'person', as every engineer is a person. To capture what a maintenance engineer does, a role restriction can say that any member is the bearer of an engineering role, and what the role amounts to will be represented by the kinds of activities that count as realizations of the role.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An entity that is a concrete implementation of a 'specified' entity.

primitiveRationale.value: The notion of concrete implementation, of providing an actual item to serve a design, is not defined. An explication should be given in terms of systems engineering practices.

example.value: A serial-numbered artefact installed at a tag, a function of a concrete artefact, a run or execution of a designed process, an industrial facility "as built".

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An object comprising one or more persons who identify by some name and pursue a common set of plans and objectives.

primitiveRationale.value: A comprehensive definition of the notion of organization is beyond the scope of IDO (it would determine which kinds of aggregations of people, and which kinds of roles, are appropriate for organizations).

example.value: A standards body, a service team, a workgroup, a company

comment.undefined: Organization is a class in the IOF Core ontology.

usageNote.value: Use OWL property chains to enforce constraints on how the bearer needs to participate in order for an activity to count as a realization.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A dependent entity that can potentially be realised through participation in an activity.

primitiveRationale.value: The necessary condition for being a 'potential' is that activity type and participation type are provided. It is however not clear what sufficient conditions would consist in, as these would at least involve a notion of successful execution for the relevant activities, which goes beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The sealing function of a gasket, the disposition of a steel bar to corrode.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000017 is 'realizable entity'.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A temporal entity that extends in space and time.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of the notion of spacetime goes beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The spatiotemporal region occupied by the fluid contents of a vessel over a period of time, the spatiotemporal region occupied by a pipeline inspection device (pig) during an inspection.

comment.undefined: An 'activity' occupies a 'spatiotemporal region'. Note that the spatiotemporal region doesn't need to be connected.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A potential whose realization is dependent on an object's structure.

primitiveRationale.value: Making the notion of "dependence on an object's structure" fully precise is beyond the scope of IDO.

scopeNote.value: An object may not lose a disposition without undergoing physical change.

example.value: The disposition of a steel bar to corrode, the disposition of a gasket to leak. The disposition to overflow a memory buffer.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000016 is 'disposition'.

comment.en: A manufacturing process or procedure (the 'M' in Ishikawa's Method). Represents the specified activities and steps in manufacturing. In die-casting (dg:Process), this includes operations like melting, injection, cooling, and demolding. Methods can be decomposed into sub-methods and have Machines and Materials as participants.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A spatial location that has zero dimension with no proper parts.

primitiveRationale.value: A comprehensive account of geometries is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The location of a probe at a given point in time

definition.value: A quality of an entity. An aspect of an entity that is intrinsic to and cannot exist without the entity.

comment.en: A quality of an entity. An aspect of an entity that is intrinsic to and cannot exist without the entity.

Actual machine instance, the instance-layer counterpart of Machine. Represents specific physical equipment with serial numbers, operational history, and sensor data. In die-casting (dga ontology), examples include specific die-casting cells (e.g., 'Cell_TCG_200'), furnaces, and robots with concrete observation data.

Raw materials, consumables, and inputs to manufacturing (the 'M' in Ishikawa's Material). Represents specified material types that participate in Methods. In die-casting (dg:Material), this includes aluminum alloys, die lubricants, release agents, and other consumables. Materials can have properties monitored via Signals and associated quality Metrics.

Abstract superclass for actual/instance layer counterparts of IshikawaItem. Integrates SOSA: each ActualIshikawaItem is a FeatureOfInterest that can be observed, has ObservableProperties, and can have FailureOccurrences. In die-casting (dga ontology), concrete instances include specific die-casting cells, material batches, and production runs.

An ordered, reusable container of CountermeasureSteps within the specified layer. Modelled as a linked list (mqto:first / mqto:rest) so that step order is external to the step instances, allowing steps to be shared across different Countermeasure procedures. The list is terminated by mqto:nil.

Manufacturing equipment, tools, and devices (the 'M' in Ishikawa's Machine). Represents specified physical resources that participate in Methods. In die-casting, dg:Aggregate represents machines like die-casting cells, furnaces, and robots. Machines provide Signals (sensor readings, control channels) and can have associated Failures and Metrics.

usageNote.value: Most industrial assets are both 'system' and 'inanimate physical object'. When modelling, e.g., a pump system, that system will be an 'inanimate physical object', with the pump part related to the pump system by 'has functional part'. The domain and range restrictions on 'has functional part' then imply that the pump system has type 'system', and the pump has type 'functional object'.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A 'functional object' with one or more functional parts whose functions contribute to its function.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of the nature of dependency between the function of a system, as a whole, and the functions of its parts is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: An electrical network, an HVAC system, a pumping system, a compressor.

comment.undefined: The SSN definition of 'System' (2017): "System is a unit of abstraction for pieces of infrastructure that implement Procedures. A System may have components, its subsystems, which are other Systems."

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A temporal region whose only parts are one or more intervals in time.

primitiveRationale.value: (This class could be considered non-primitive, defined in terms of 'temporal region' and each part mapping by 'has temporal extent' to 'interval'.)

example.value: Intermittent times of operation of a heater, maintenance periods at a plant, times that a milling machine is run, The Saturdays of 2011, the construction period for a plant modification

comment.undefined: BFO_0000038 is 'one-dimensional temporal region' in BFO-ISO.

comment.en: Actual material instance, the instance-layer counterpart of Material. Represents specific batches, lots, or quantities of material with identifiers, composition data, and quality measurements. In die-casting (dga ontology), examples include specific aluminum alloy batches with batch numbers, chemical composition, and temperature observations.

definition.value: System is a unit of abstraction for pieces of infrastructure that implement Procedures. A System may have components, its subsystems, which are other systems.

comment.en: System is a unit of abstraction for pieces of infrastructure that implement Procedures. A System may have components, its subsystems, which are other systems.

definition.value: Device, agent (including humans), or software (simulation) involved in, or implementing, a Procedure. Sensors respond to a stimulus, e.g., a change in the environment, or input data composed from the results of prior Observations, and generate a Result. Sensors can be hosted by Platforms.

example.value: Accelerometers, gyroscopes, barometers, magnetometers, and so forth are Sensors that are typically mounted on a modern smart phone (which acts as Platform). Other examples of sensors include the human eyes.

comment.en: Device, agent (including humans), or software (simulation) involved in, or implementing, a Procedure. Sensors respond to a stimulus, e.g., a change in the environment, or input data composed from the results of prior Observations, and generate a Result. Sensors can be hosted by Platforms.

A remediation procedure that mitigates one or more Failures. Countermeasures are structured as ordered sequences of CountermeasureSteps, represented via the mqto:List pattern for reusability. As a subclass of pko:Procedure, countermeasures align with the Procedural Knowledge Ontology's procedure modeling patterns while maintaining integration with the LIS specified/actual architecture.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A potential, the realization of which is of value for the bearer.

primitiveRationale.value: Accounting for  (mental) attitudes of value is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: A plan to act, an approval of a survey.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An entity that is part of time or takes place in time.

primitiveRationale.value: A comprehensive account of time, and of the unfolding of events in time, is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: A moment in time, a period of time, an activity or process, a beginning, a closing event.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000003 is 'occurrent'.

definition.value: A device that is used by, or implements, an (Actuation) Procedure that changes the state of the world.

example.value: A window actuator for automatic window control, i.e., opening or closing the window.

comment.en: A device that is used by, or implements, an (Actuation) Procedure that changes the state of the world.

definition.value: An event in the real world that 'triggers' the Sensor. The properties associated to the Stimulus may be different to the eventual observed ObservableProperty. It is the event, not the object, that triggers the Sensor.

comment.en: An event in the real world that 'triggers' the Sensor. The properties associated to the Stimulus may be different to the eventual observed ObservableProperty. It is the event, not the object, that triggers the Sensor.

Actual execution of a Method, the instance-layer counterpart of Method. Links to concrete PhysicalMachines and MaterialChunks as participants. In die-casting (dga ontology), this represents actual production shots with specific machines, material batches, timestamps, and observation data. Note: ProcessExecution does not directly subclass lis:Activity (which is lis:Specified), but instead subclasses ActualIshikawaItem (lis:Actual) and implements Method (which is lis:Activity). This maintains the LIS distinction between specified and actual layers.

A specified data channel in the electrical-engineering sense: a named, typed signal such as a PLC variable, an analog voltage line, a fieldbus message, or a time-series data stream. A Signal may carry measurement data (sensor/output signals), control data (actuator/input signals), or both simultaneously — a setpoint, for instance, is written as a command to an actuator and can equally be read back as an observable value. It describes the metadata of the channel (identifier, unit, sampling rate, direction, etc.) rather than any particular observation or sensor device. It sits in the specified layer as the concrete data-source or data-sink binding for an abstract Metric, and is thereby distinct from sosa:ObservableProperty (which characterises an observable quality of a feature of interest in the actual layer) and from sosa:Sensor (which is the physical observing device).

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A temporal entity in which one or more physical objects participate.

primitiveRationale.value: (Here we could consider saying the class is in fact not primitive, but defined in terms of 'temporal' and 'has participant'.)

example.value: A pumping, an inspection, a report writing, a fastening (the activity of a bolt in holding two pieces together).

scopeNote.value: An 'activity' doesn't need to be temporally connected. For example, intermittent operation of a heater over a week can be represented by a single 'activity' individual; it will occupy a non-connected 'interval region'. The details of each connected activity part ("singular activities") may be modelled by relating the activity to 'interval' individuals.

comment.undefined: ISO 15926-2:2003, 4.7.17 states, "... activities are considered to be space-time extensions in which other individuals and events participate."

usageNote.value: Typically, a physical quantity of a participant, such as a "pressure" individual, is selected, and the "pressure profile" of the activity is introduced. The relation 'profile of quality' links to that pressure quality individual that defines (is the basis for abstraction to) the activity profile. By means of the relation 'profile quantity datum', the activity profile can be related to 'quantity datum' individuals, to which aggregation can be applied, giving a basis for classification of the activity profile; for example, as a "rising pressure" activity.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An activity obtained by "selective abstraction" to just that part of an activity that consists in varying or maintaining a specified 'quality' of a participant.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of the notion of selective abstraction is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The BFO 2.0 reference guide provides good examples, like "constant speed profile", "acceleration profile", and "irregular beat profile".
An 'activity profile' of an 'activity' involving rotating equipment, instantiating a class "100 revolutions per minute".

comment.undefined: An 'activity profile' is a part of an 'activity', a "selective abstraction" (using a term from the BFO reference guide) made according to a quality of interest to the process. Typically, we will select a physical quantity such as pressure or temperature, and refer to the "pressure profile" of the activity. By means of the relation 'profile quantity datum', the activity profile can be related to a set of 'quantity datum' individuals, to which aggregation can be applied, giving a basis for classification.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A temporal entity that extends only in time.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of the notion of time goes beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The moment a valve is closed, upstart time for a motor, the length of time that a bolt has been installed, intervals between maintenance.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000008 is 'temporal region'

note.value: Many observations may be created via the same Procedure, the same way as many tables are assembled using the same instructions (as information objects, not their concrete realization).

definition.value: A workflow, protocol, plan, algorithm, or computational method specifying how to make an Observation, create a Sample, or make a change to the state of the world (via an Actuator). A Procedure is re-usable, and might be involved in many Observations, Samplings, or Actuations. It explains the steps to be carried out to arrive at reproducible results.

example.value: The measured wind speed differs depending on the height of the sensor above the surface, e.g., due to friction. Consequently, procedures for measuring wind speed define a standard height for anemometers above ground, typically 10m for meteorological measures and 2m in Agrometeorology. This definition of height, sensor placement, and so forth are defined by the Procedure.

comment.en: A workflow, protocol, plan, algorithm, or computational method specifying how to make an Observation, create a Sample, or make a change to the state of the world (via an Actuator). A Procedure is re-usable, and might be involved in many Observations, Samplings, or Actuations. It explains the steps to be carried out to arrive at reproducible results.

usageNote.value: As with any 'potential', the existence of a role is dependent on its bearer. A particular meeting room's role may therefore not be transferred to a different room; a different role individual with similar classification would be needed.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A potential that is assigned to an object.

primitiveRationale.value: Making the notion of assignment (to a task) fully precise is beyond the scope of IDO. With complex intentions notions of obligation, which are often required in role assignments, this is particularly clear.

scopeNote.value: This class is intended to serve the same purpose as the ISO 15926-2:2003 'role' entity type, as well as the same-named BFO class (BFO_0000023).

example.value: The role an individual person takes in a work situation, such as a manager. A student role.
The role of an interior space (a 'site') as a meeting room is realized when it serves as the space for conducting meetings. The site may be assigned a storage room role at a different time.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000023 is 'role'.

note.value: A transient sample, such as a ships-track or flight-line, might be identified and described, but is unlikely to be revisited exactly.

definition.value: Feature which is intended to be representative of a FeatureOfInterest on which Observations may be made.

example.value: A 'station' is essentially an identifiable locality where a sensor system or Procedure may be deployed and an observation made. In the context of the observation model, it connotes the 'world in the vicinity of the station', so the observed properties relate to the physical medium at the station, and not to any physical artifact such as a mooring, buoy, benchmark, monument, well, etc.

A statistical sample is often designed to be characteristic of an entire population, so that observations can be made regarding the sample that provide a good estimate of the properties of the population.

comment.en: A Sample is the result from an act of Sampling.

usageNote.value: As with 'spatiotemporal region', a spatial location need not be connected; for example, a location may consist in several cube-shaped regions.

The subclasses 'point in space', 'line in space', 'plane in space', and 'volume in space' are all defined as singular: for example, a 'point in space' individual may not have multiple points as parts. This, in contrast to the analogous BFO classes 'one-dimensional spatial region', etc., which are all defined as plural, allowing for disconnected parts.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A location defined relative to a spatial reference system.

primitiveRationale.value: To provide an account of space, or spatial reference system, is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The cube-shaped region extending 1 metre in each direction from origo, the line extending 10 metres from origo along the z axis, two points with coordinates on a two-dimensional plane; the location of a container at 10:00 AM.

scopeNote.value: The position of a 'spatial location' is fixed (relative to the frame of reference); it is not dependent on changing positions of 'physical object's.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000006 is 'spatial region'.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A physical object that is a contiguous, non-separable part of a physical object.

explanatoryNote.value: BFO_0000024 is 'fiat object part'. This is at least very close in meaning to 'feature' as defined in in ISO 15926.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of what "non-separable" means is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: A flange face, a connection port (terminal), the inside surface of a pump, a painted area of a manifold, a bolt threading.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A temporal region whose only parts are one or more points in time.

primitiveRationale.value: (Could be considered non-primitive, defined in terms of 'temporal region' and each part mapping by 'has temporal extent' to 'instant'.)

example.value: The moment a valve is closed, the instants of a series of measurements.

comment.undefined: obo:BFO_0000148 is 'zero-dimensional temporal region'

comment.en: The actual execution of a Countermeasure procedure, performed to mitigate one or more FailureOccurrences. Links to the Countermeasure specification via lis:implements (LIS pattern) and tracks which specific failures were addressed. As a subclass of pko:ProcedureExecution, countermeasure executions align with PKO's procedure execution model while maintaining LIS actual/specified semantics. Note: Does not directly subclass lis:Activity (which is lis:Specified), but implements Countermeasure (which is lis:Activity), maintaining the LIS distinction between specified and actual layers.

definition.value: An observable quality (property, characteristic) of a FeatureOfInterest.

example.value: The height of a tree, the depth of a water body, or the temperature of a surface are examples of observable properties, while the value of a classic car is not (directly) observable but asserted.

comment.en: An observable quality (property, characteristic) of a FeatureOfInterest.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A quality that can be quantified by measurement.

primitiveRationale.value: It is beyond the scope of IDO to give an account of measurement.

example.value: The mass of a bar of steel, the temperature of a medium, the inner diameter of a pipe, the lift height of a crane, the head (lift height) of a pump.

comment.undefined: DOLCE defines 'physical-quality' as, "A quality inherent in a physical endurant."

definition.value: A Platform is an entity that hosts other entities, particularly Sensors, Actuators, Samplers, and other Platforms.

example.value: A post, buoy, vehicle, ship, aircraft, satellite, cell-phone, human or animal may act as platforms for (technical or biological) sensors or actuators.

comment.en: A Platform is an entity that hosts other entities, particularly Sensors, Actuators, Samplers, and other Platforms.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A spatial location that is a two-dimensional plane.

primitiveRationale.value: A comprehensive account of geometries is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The midway horizontal plane of a volume given by coordinates, the plane of a moving work platform at 12:00

definition.value: The thing whose property is being estimated or calculated in the course of an Observation to arrive at a Result or whose property is being manipulated by an Actuator, or which is being sampled or transformed in an act of Sampling.

example.value: When measuring the height of a tree, the height is the observed ObservableProperty, 20m may be the Result of the Observation, and the tree is the FeatureOfInterest. A window is a FeatureOfInterest for an automatic window control Actuator.

comment.en: The thing whose property is being estimated or calculated in the course of an Observation to arrive at a Result or whose property is being manipulated by an Actuator, or which is being sampled or transformed in an act of Sampling.

usageNote.value: A common application of 'information object' is in representing facts about the vocabulary of the ontology itself. The OWL facility of "punning" treats OWL classes and properties as OWL individuals, enabling OWL object properties to be applied to them. In use cases that involve punning, the SKOS vocabulary is recommended to be used, with the SKOS classes 'concept', 'concept scheme', and 'collection'  as subclasses of 'information object'.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An object that captures information about something.

primitiveRationale.value: The notion of aboutness is not defined.

example.value: A report, a drawing, a P&ID diagram, a purchase order, a certificate, a database, a database record, a measurement datum, a unit of measure, an identifier, an algorithm, a programming language.

scopeNote.value: The BFO class 'generically dependent continuant' represents the notion of a pattern that exists in at least one copy, e.g., "the sequence of this protein molecule". This is more general than 'information object', and not at present included in IDO.  'information object' is a subclass, with the added condition that the pattern 'is about' something.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000031 is 'generically dependent continuant'.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: A 'quality' that can be characterised in terms of material phase.

primitiveRationale.value: A precise account of material phase is beyond the scope of IDO.

example.value: The liquid quality of an amount of molten steel, the solid quality of a steel gasket.

definition.value: Any information that is provided to a Procedure for its use.

comment.en: Any information that is provided to a Procedure for its use.

usageNote.value: A common need is to relate a measure based on a 'physical quantity' to an 'activity', like average speed to a transport activity. This may not be implemented with an 'average speed' entity that is dependent on the transport. For a suitable representation pattern, see the class 'activity profile' and related resources. A "shortcut" approach may use the relation 'has participant quality quantified as'.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: An entity that is an inherent quality or realizable potential of an object.

primitiveRationale.value: Providing necessary and sufficient conditions for dependence is beyond the scope of IDO. Note that the BFO analogue, 'specifically dependent continuant', is defined in terms of the primitive relation 'specifically depends on' (s-depends on), which is not included in IDO.

example.value: The colour of a surface, the temperature of a medium, a technical contact role, the storage-space role of a room.

scopeNote.value: Any 'object' will be the bearer of various 'dependent' entities, but any 'temporal' entity has only an indirect relationship to 'dependent's. This is in line with BFO.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000020 is 'specifically dependent continuant'.

comment.en: Relates an ObservableProperty to the MetricAssessment that evaluated it (actual layer). Inverse of mqto:evaluatesProperty. Counterpart to mqto:readBy in the specified layer.

usageNote.value: For annotations, see the inverse relation 'has participant'.

An asset model with 'implements' relationships supports compatibility checks for implementing objects, to uncover possible violations of requirements.  This is requires a transformation of the asset model, as follows: replace each 'implements'/'implementedBy' relationship with identity (owl:sameAs), and replace classifications as 'specified' or 'actual' with just 'object' for the participant individuals.  Any semantic inconsistency between the the specified object and the implementing object may then be discovered by OWL reasoning.

Periods of 'implements' relationships should be recorded using OWL annotations, to enable prescriptive objects to be matched with installed objects for selected points or periods in time. A detailed representation using 'role' is also possible.
The mechanism for checking requirement consistency on 'implements' relationships must be implemented as a transformation that is external to the OWL ontology.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x 'implements' y, then x is a 'specified' entity, y is an 'actual' entity, and at some time, x is the same individual as y.

explanatoryNote.value: The domain and range restrictions to disjunct classes 'actual' and 'specified' prevent chains of 'implements' relationships.

primitiveRationale.value: 'implements' is used to express identity relationships that may vary over time. Because OWL lacks time indices, the representation is informal.

scopeNote.value: The 'implements' relation is central to the intended use of IDO. It allows for a record of different individuals being installed or otherwise serving as a specified object over time. Typically, a serial-numbered physical artefact is installed as a "specification" individual (a "tag") at one time, and later replaced with another serial-numbered physical artefact. The 'implements' relation carries semantic restrictions only to ensure no object is both Actual and Specified. No further constraints are given, to allow 'implements' relationships even where restrictions on the specification object are inconsistent with those on the implementing (installed) object.

example.value: Pump P-101 is a tag, and ACME pumps with serial numbers 457636 and 457711 implement (have been installed as) this pump.

usageNote.value: An asset model with 'implements' relationships supports compatibility checks for implementing objects, to uncover possible violations of requirements.  This is requires a transformation of the asset model, as follows: replace each 'implements'/'implementedBy' relationship with identity (owl:sameAs), and replace classifications as 'specified' or 'actual' with just 'object' for the participant individuals.  Any semantic inconsistency between the the specified object and the implementing object may then be discovered by OWL reasoning.

The mechanism for checking requirement consistency on 'implements' relationships must be implemented as a transformation that is external to the OWL ontology.

The 'implements' relation carries semantic restrictions only to ensure no object is both Actual and Specified. example.value: Pump P-101 is a tag, and ACME pumps with serial numbers 457636 and 457711 implement (have been installed as) this pump.

definition.value: Relation between a Property and the entity it belongs to.

comment.en: Relation between a Property and the entity it belongs to.
Relates a Signal (data channel) to the Metric that is computed from it (specified layer). Inverse of mqto:readsFrom. For the actual-layer counterpart see mqto:evaluatedBy.

definition.value: A relation between some aspect of an entity and a Property.

example.value: For example, from a Sensor to the properties it can observe; from an Actuator to the properties it can act on; from a Deployment to the properties it was installed to observe or act on; from a SystemCapability to the Property the capability is described for.

comment.en: A relation between some aspect of an entity and a Property.

usageNote.value: For annotations, see the inverse relation 'has material part'.

definition.value: Relation between a Property and the entity it belongs to.

comment.en: Relation between a Property and the entity it belongs to.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x 'has activity part' y, then x and y are activities, and x 'has arranged part' y.

example.value: The startup period of a chemical process, the inspection part of a maintenance activity, the moment a software program terminates execution.

scopeNote.value: Parts of activities may be determined by a range of criteria, including temporal restrictions (to sub-periods), spatial restrictions (parts that occupy sub-volumes of space), by the engagement of participants (temporal or spatial parts where one or more participants are active), and "selective abstraction" to profiles (parts determined by that which affects variation in selected qualities).

comment.undefined: BFO_0000117 is 'has occurrent part' in BFO-ISO. This relation has 'occurrent' as domain and range, where the IDO 'has activity part' is restricted to just 'activity'.

usageNote.value: For annotations, see the inverse relation 'has activity part'.

comment.en: Relates a Metric to the Signal (data channel) it is computed from (specified layer). Inverse of mqto:readBy. For the actual-layer counterpart see mqto:evaluatesProperty.

usageNote.value: An asset model with 'implements' relationships supports compatibility checks for implementing objects, to uncover possible violations of requirements.  This is requires a transformation of the asset model, as follows: replace each 'implements'/'implementedBy' relationship with identity (owl:sameAs), and replace classifications as 'specified' or 'actual' with just 'object' for the participant individuals.  Any semantic inconsistency between the the specified object and the implementing object may then be discovered by OWL reasoning.

Periods of 'implements' relationships should be recorded using OWL annotations, to enable prescriptive objects to be matched with installed objects for selected points or periods in time. A detailed representation using 'role' is also possible.
The mechanism for checking requirement consistency on 'implements' relationships must be implemented as a transformation that is external to the OWL ontology.

explanatoryNote.value: The domain and range restrictions to disjunct classes 'actual' and 'specified' prevent chains of 'implements' relationships.

primitiveRationale.value: 'implements' is used to express identity relationships that may vary over time. Because OWL lacks time indices, the representation is informal.

Typically, a serial-numbered physical artefact is installed as a "specification" individual (a "tag") at one time, and later replaced with another serial-numbered physical artefact. The 'implements' relation carries semantic restrictions only to ensure no object is both Actual and Specified. No further constraints are given, to allow 'implements' relationships even where restrictions on the specification object are inconsistent with those on the implementing (installed) object.

example.value: Pump P-101 is a tag, and ACME pumps with serial numbers 457636 and 457711 implement (have been installed as) this pump.

comment.en: Relates a MetricAssessment to the ObservableProperty it evaluated (actual layer). Counterpart to mqto:readsFrom, which relates a Metric to a Signal in the specified layer.

usageNote.value: Assume y is an 'activity profile', obtained from some containing activity z by selective abstraction on a quality q of a participant in z. Then, relate y by 'profile quantity datum' to magnitudes of q that are recorded during the execution of y.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x has 'profile quantity datum' y, then x is an 'activity profile', and x 'has participant quality quantified as' y.

example.value: The pressure of a liquid is measured at 10, 11, and 12 bar at different points of time during a transport activity.

comment.undefined: Intuitively, 'profile quantity datum' should only point to quantity data recorded while the activity is taking place. This can not be enforced with OWL restrictions on the relation (it would require a "diamond" pattern). However, with a suitable date or time data property value restriction, an individual activity can constrain related quantity data.

usageNote.value: For annotations, see the inverse relation 'has spatial extent'.
For annotations, see the inverse relation 'has participant'.
For annotations, see the inverse relation 'resides in'.
For annotations, see the inverse relation 'has arranged part'.
For annotations, see the inverse relation 'profile of quality'.
For annotations, see the inverse relation 'is about'.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: x 'has physical quantity' y if and only if x 'has quality' y, and y is a 'physical quantity'.

example.value: A section of pipe has length, a crane has lifting power, a flange has inner diameter.

usageNote.value: When introducing shortcut properties as subproperties, annotations should be provided to explain the intended quantity type, any quantification type (e.g., measured, nominal, etc.), and unit of measure.
This relation is suitable as the top node in a taxonomy of "shortcut" relations that combine quality and unit of measure into a single, simple data property.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x has 'quality quantity value' y, then x is an 'object', and y is a literal value that quantifies a quality of x.

example.value: Mass in kilograms, pressure in barg.

usageNote.value: For annotations, see the inverse relation 'has functional part'.
The 'site' of an object will occupy different 'spatial location's over time if the location and size of the object changes.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x 'has spatial extent' y, then x is a 'physical object', and y is the 'spatial location' determined as the extension in space of x.

primitiveRationale.value: The notion of  "spatial extension" of an object is not fully defined.

example.value: The volume occupied by an process skid, the volume occupied by a volume of flammable gas.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000210 is 'occupies spatial region at some time' (from BFO-ISO)

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: x 'has interest' y if and only if x is an 'organization' or 'organism', x 'has potential' y, and y is an 'interest'.

example.value: An intention to start a process, a plan to modify a plant, an approval of a certification.

comment.undefined: Note, the use of "potential" in the definition does not imply "not actual", only "not necessarily actual".

usageNote.value: This relation is suitable as the top node in a taxonomy of "shortcut" relations that relate objects directly to quality datums, leaving the quality implicit.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x 'has quality quantified as' y, then x is an 'object', and y is a 'quality datum' that 'quantifies quality' a 'quality' of x.

example.value: The GeoSPARQL object property geo:hasLength relates (spatial) objects to datums that provide length with number and unit of measure. lis:hasQualityQuantifiedAs is suitable as a superproperty in an alignment with GeoSPARQL data that includes geo:hasLength relationships.

usageNote.value: For annotations, see the inverse relation 'has physical quantity'.

comment.en: Relates an ObservableProperty to the MetricAssessment that evaluated it (actual layer). Inverse of mqto:evaluatesProperty. Counterpart to mqto:readBy in the specified layer.

usageNote.value: For annotations, see the inverse relation 'realized in'.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000055 is 'realizes' in BFO-ISO.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x 'has activity profile' y, then x 'has activity part' y, and y is determined by selective abstraction to that part of x that effects variation in some quality of a participant in x.

primitiveRationale.value: The notion of selective abstraction is not defined.

example.value: A pipeline transport activity has a "profile" part which is the variation in fluid pressure.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000133 is 'process profile of', in the Molecular Process Ontology and other BFO-based ontologies.

usageNote.value: For annotations, see the inverse relation 'has ending'.
This relation is suitable as the top node in a taxonomy of relations of relative temporal location.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x 'occurs relative to' y, then x and y are 'temporal' entities, with temporal location.

primitiveRationale.value: The notion of relative location in time is introduced with this relation.

example.value: A vessel is emptied before inspection, a valve is opened before draining of a pipeline, firmware is installed before the equipment begins to operate.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x is 'realized in' y, then x is a 'potential', and y is an 'activity' that successfully realizes x.

primitiveRationale.value: A full account of the notion of "successfully realizes" is beyond the scope of IDO. It depends on an account of "success", in that failed activities will not be realizations, and on the bearer of the potential participating in the realizing activity in the right way.

example.value: A pump realizes its function in a pumping (activity), a bolt realizes its function in a connection, a software monitoring application realizes its function in streaming sensor data.

comment.undefined: BFO_0000054 is 'has realization' in BFO-ISO, and 'realized in' in the Relations Ontology.

An asset model with 'implements' relationships supports compatibility checks for implementing objects, to uncover possible violations of requirements.  This is requires a transformation of the asset model, as follows: replace each 'implements'/'implementedBy' relationship with identity (owl:sameAs), and replace classifications as 'specified' or 'actual' with just 'object' for the participant individuals.  Any semantic inconsistency between the the specified object and the implementing object may then be discovered by OWL reasoning.

Periods of 'implements' relationships should be recorded using OWL annotations, to enable prescriptive objects to be matched with installed objects for selected points or periods in time. A detailed representation using 'role' is also possible.
The mechanism for checking requirement consistency on 'implements' relationships must be implemented as a transformation that is external to the OWL ontology.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x 'implements' y, then x is a 'specified' entity, y is an 'actual' entity, and at some time, x is the same individual as y.

explanatoryNote.value: The domain and range restrictions to disjunct classes 'actual' and 'specified' prevent chains of 'implements' relationships.

primitiveRationale.value: 'implements' is used to express identity relationships that may vary over time. Because OWL lacks time indices, the representation is informal.

scopeNote.value: The 'implements' relation is central to the intended use of IDO. It allows for a record of different individuals being installed or otherwise serving as a specified object over time. Typically, a serial-numbered physical artefact is installed as a "specification" individual (a "tag") at one time, and later replaced with another serial-numbered physical artefact. The 'implements' relation carries semantic restrictions only to ensure no object is both Actual and Specified. No further constraints are given, to allow 'implements' relationships even where restrictions on the specification object are inconsistent with those on the implementing (installed) object.

example.value: Pump P-101 is a tag, and ACME pumps with serial numbers 457636 and 457711 implement (have been installed as) this pump.

example.value: A regulation describes a procedure, a datum quantifies a quality, a nameplate provides basic information about a pressure vessel, a log file traces quantity values during an activity.

scopeNote.value: This relation is suitable as the top node in a taxonomy of relations between information objects and entities, including describing, naming, characterisation, and evaluation.

usageNote.value: 'has functional part' is used to represent the functional break-down of a system. Where x is a functional part of a system y, the realisation of one or more functions of x contributes to the performance of y.

If 'has part' relationship is modeled between Function/Disposition instances, 'has functional part' can be inferred between the objects that are bearers of the respective functions/dispositions if the corresponding rules are implemented.

'has functional part' provides a compact way of modeling that is not expressive enough to model multiple functional breakdowns. Note that modeling 'has part' directly between Function/Disposition instances gives a higher flexibility and precision, in particular when objects have multiple functions or dispositions.
Note that, for physical objects, 'has functional part' is a specialization of 'has arranged part'.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x 'has functional part' y, then x is a 'system' of which y is a part, and y has one or more functions that contribute to the function of x.

primitiveRationale.value: The notion of "contributing function" is not fully accounted for in IDO.

example.value: A separation system has a cooling system as functional part.

There are two main, and very different, uses of 'concretizes' for an information object y.  (1) The information object is made concrete in printed or electronic copies. Meta-property that specifies which data property of sosa:Result instances should be extracted and used as input for this Metric's calculation. For example, if a Metric's resultProperty points to dga:measValue, the metric calculation will extract the measValue field from each Result instance within the specified time window. If it points to dga:diameter, it extracts the diameter values instead. This enables multiple metrics to derive different calculations from the same observation results by selecting different result attributes. Note: This is a meta-level construct (an object property pointing to data properties), which while not ideal OWL modeling practice, provides a simple declarative mechanism for metrics to specify their input data field without requiring complex extraction rules or SPARQL property paths.

usageNote.value: An asset model with 'implements' relationships supports compatibility checks for implementing objects, to uncover possible violations of requirements.  This is requires a transformation of the asset model, as follows: replace each 'implements'/'implementedBy' relationship with identity (owl:sameAs), and replace classifications as 'specified' or 'actual' with just 'object' for the participant individuals.  Any semantic inconsistency between the the specified object and the implementing object may then be discovered by OWL reasoning.

Periods of 'implements' relationships should be recorded using OWL annotations, to enable prescriptive objects to be matched with installed objects for selected points or periods in time. A detailed representation using 'role' is also possible.
The mechanism for checking requirement consistency on 'implements' relationships must be implemented as a transformation that is external to the OWL ontology.

explanatoryNote.value: The domain and range restrictions to disjunct classes 'actual' and 'specified' prevent chains of 'implements' relationships.

scopeNote.value: The 'implements' relation is central to the intended use of IDO. It allows for a record of different individuals being installed or otherwise serving as a specified object over time. The 'implements' relation carries semantic restrictions only to ensure no object is both Actual and Specified. No further constraints are given, to allow 'implements' relationships even where restrictions on the specification object are inconsistent with those on the implementing (installed) object.

example.value: Pump P-101 is a tag, and ACME pumps with serial numbers 457636 and 457711 implement (have been installed as) this pump.

example.value: A hammer is the tool in a nail-driving activity, a pressure transmitter outputs data in a monitoring activity, a maintenance engineer replaces an instrument in a repair activity, a light sensor controls the level of illumination.

definition.value: The result time is the instant of time when the Observation, Actuation or Sampling activity was completed.

comment.en: The result time is the instant of time when the Observation, Actuation or Sampling activity was completed.
A countermeasure execution was suggested by a failure occurrence.

usageNote.value: An asset model with 'implements' relationships supports compatibility checks for implementing objects, to uncover possible violations of requirements.  This is requires a transformation of the asset model, as follows: replace each 'implements'/'implementedBy' relationship with identity (owl:sameAs), and replace classifications as 'specified' or 'actual' with just 'object' for the participant individuals.  Any semantic inconsistency between the the specified object and the implementing object may then be discovered by OWL reasoning.

Periods of 'implements' relationships should be recorded using OWL annotations, to enable prescriptive objects to be matched with installed objects for selected points or periods in time. A detailed representation using 'role' is also possible.
The mechanism for checking requirement consistency on 'implements' relationships must be implemented as a transformation that is external to the OWL ontology.

naturalLanguageDefinition.value: If x 'implements' y, then x is a 'specified' entity, y is an 'actual' entity, and at some time, x is the same individual as y.

explanatoryNote.value: The domain and range restrictions to disjunct classes 'actual' and 'specified' prevent chains of 'implements' relationships.

primitiveRationale.value: 'implements' is used to express identity relationships that may vary over time. Because OWL lacks time indices, the representation is informal.

scopeNote.value: The 'implements' relation is central to the intended use of IDO. It allows for a record of different individuals being installed or otherwise serving as a specified object over time. Typically, a serial-numbered physical artefact is installed as a "specification" individual (a "tag") at one time, and later replaced with another serial-numbered physical artefact. The 'implements' relation carries semantic restrictions only to ensure no object is both Actual and Specified. No further constraints are given, to allow 'implements' relationships even where restrictions on the specification object are inconsistent with those on the implementing (installed) object.

example.value: Pump P-101 is a tag, and ACME pumps with serial numbers 457636 and 457711 implement (have been installed as) this pump.

comment.en: A countermeasure execution mitigated a failure occurrence. Note: this is the actual-layer forward property (past participle). The specified-layer equivalent uses mqto:mitigates (active voice). The naming difference is intentional: 'mitigated' records a completed fact at instance level, while 'mitigates' expresses a general capability at schema level.
